text:
HEMATOLOGY/ONCOLOGY FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Patient: Robert Delacroix | DOB: [on file] | MRN: [on file]
Attending: [Attending Oncologist]
Visit Type: Established Patient — Prostate Cancer Follow-Up

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Follow-up for metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC) on olaparib (Lynparza). Dose reduction in place following grade 3 anemia approximately 2 weeks prior. Here today for reassessment of labs, symptom review, and review of recent PSMA PET/CT.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Mr. Delacroix is a 71-year-old gentleman with a well-established history of high-risk metastatic prostate adenocarcinoma (Gleason 4+5=9, Grade Group 5, cT3bN1M1b at diagnosis) who carries a pathogenic germline BRCA2 variant (c.5946delT). His disease course has been marked by polyostotic bone metastases from the outset, initial excellent response to ADT plus abiraterone/prednisone (Zytiga), subsequent CRPC transition with PCWG3-defined biochemical progression at approximately 18 months, then a period of disease control on enzalutamide (Xtandi) with PSA nadir of 0.6 ng/mL before radiographic progression with new thoracic spine lesions and a right 4th rib metastasis at roughly 9 months on enzalutamide. He received palliative EBRT to T10-T12 (20 Gy in 5 fractions) for epidural extension/pain, and olaparib (Lynparza) 300 mg BID was initiated approximately 4 months ago in the context of biallelic BRCA2 loss.

Approximately 10 weeks into olaparib therapy, he developed symptomatic grade 3 anemia (Hgb nadir 7.8 g/dL) with dyspnea on exertion and marked fatigue requiring 2 units pRBCs. Olaparib was held briefly and then restarted at a reduced dose of 200 mg BID per standard prescribing guidelines for grade 3/4 hematologic toxicity. He has since required ongoing transfusion support — he has received a total of 4 pRBC transfusions over the past approximately 3 months. Despite the myelosuppression, the oncologic trajectory has been favorable: PSA has continued to trend downward (from 9.2 ng/mL pre-olaparib to 1.4 ng/mL today), and a Ga-68 PSMA PET/CT performed earlier this month demonstrated marked decrease in PSMA avidity throughout known osseous metastases, with no new visceral or nodal disease identified.

He reports persistent fatigue, currently grade 2, which he describes as a "bone-deep tiredness" that limits his activity but does not preclude self-care. He is no longer able to take his daily walks of more than a block or two. Appetite is reduced but he is maintaining his weight. Thoracic back pain has improved markedly since completing radiation — he rates it 1-2/10, using acetaminophen PRN. No new neurological symptoms; he denies any leg weakness, numbness, bowel or bladder dysfunction. Nausea is mild and largely controlled with ondansetron PRN. No fevers, chills, or infectious symptoms. No frank rectal or GI bleeding. He is on amlodipine for enzalutamide-era hypertension, which has been continued.

He is accompanied today by his wife. Both have questions about whether the ongoing anemia is expected to improve or worsen, and what the longer-term plan is given that he is tolerating the treatment but remains transfusion dependent.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS
Constitutional: Fatigue grade 2, ongoing. No significant weight loss in the past month. Appetite mildly reduced.
Cardiovascular: No chest pain, no palpitations at present. Mild bilateral lower extremity edema, unchanged from prior.
Pulmonary: Exertional dyspnea grade 1 (improved from grade 2 at time of Hgb nadir following transfusion). No cough or hemoptysis.
Gastrointestinal: Mild nausea, well-controlled on Zofran PRN. No vomiting, diarrhea, melena, or hematochezia. Constipation mild, managed with docusate.
Musculoskeletal: Thoracic back pain 1-2/10, improved post-radiation. No new joint pain or bone pain.
Neurological: Cognitive sluggishness noted as chronic (pre-dates current therapy, partially attributed to ADT). No focal neurological deficits, no seizures (relevant given prior enzalutamide exposure), no new headaches.
Genitourinary: Ongoing castrate-level testosterone side effects including hot flashes (grade 2), reduced libido. No hematuria.
Skin: No rash.
Hematologic: Ongoing transfusion requirement as above. No bruising or spontaneous bleeding.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION
Vitals: BP 138/82 mmHg, HR 78 bpm, RR 14, SpO2 97% on room air, Temp 36.7°C, Weight 88 kg (down ~3 kg from pre-olaparib baseline of 91 kg)
ECOG Performance Status: 1-2 (able to perform self-care, up >50% of waking hours)

General: Pleasant, cooperative elderly gentleman, mildly fatigued appearing. In no acute distress. Slightly pale conjunctivae consistent with anemia.
HEENT: Anicteric sclerae, mildly pale palpebral conjunctivae. Dry oral mucosa. No oral ulcerations.
Neck: No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs. Mild bilateral pitting edema to mid-shin (1+), unchanged.
Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezing or crackles.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatosplenomegaly appreciated on palpation. Normal bowel sounds.
Musculoskeletal: Midline thoracic spine non-tender to palpation over treated field. No new tenderness. Normal strength in bilateral lower extremities (4+/5 globally, unchanged from prior). Gait steady, no ataxia.
Neurological: Alert and oriented x3. Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. No focal deficits. DTRs symmetric 2+ bilaterally.
Skin: No petechiae, purpura, or ecchymoses.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS
CBC (today):
  - Hemoglobin: 9.2 g/dL (grade 2 anemia; up from nadir of 7.8 g/dL; most recent transfusion approximately 3 weeks ago)
  - WBC: 4.8 x10^9/L (within normal limits)
  - ANC: 2.4 x10^9/L (adequate)
  - Platelets: 164 x10^9/L (low-normal; down from 198 x10^9/L at olaparib initiation)
  - MCV: 88 fL (normocytic)
  - Reticulocyte count: 0.9% (low — hypoproliferative pattern)
Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
  - Creatinine: 1.1 mg/dL, eGFR 64 mL/min (stable CKD stage 2)
  - AST/ALT: 32/41 U/L (within normal limits)
  - Total bilirubin: 0.7 mg/dL
  - Alkaline phosphatase: 192 U/L (mildly elevated, stable — osseous disease contribution likely)
  - Albumin: 3.6 g/dL
PSA: 1.4 ng/mL (continued decline from 9.2 ng/mL pre-olaparib; represents approximately 85% decline from pre-olaparib value)
Testosterone: 10 ng/dL (confirmed castrate)
LDH: 178 U/L (normal)
Erythropoietin level (drawn last month): 38 mIU/mL — appropriately elevated, no EPO deficiency as contributory etiology
Iron studies (last month): Ferritin 210 ng/mL, TIBC and transferrin saturation within normal limits — no iron deficiency component

---

IMAGING RESULTS
Ga-68 PSMA PET/CT (reviewed today): Compared to the pre-olaparib PSMA PET at time of olaparib initiation and the prior bone scan showing active osseous disease, the current study demonstrates substantial decrease in PSMA avidity throughout the known osseous metastatic sites in the lumbar spine, pelvis, and right femur. The T10 and T12 lesions (post-radiation) show very low to absent PSMA uptake, consistent with treatment effect. No new PSMA-avid osseous lesions are identified. No hepatic, pulmonary, or adrenal PSMA-avid lesions. Pelvic lymph node stations are without PSMA-avid adenopathy. Overall, a very favorable metabolic response to olaparib. This was reviewed by the treating team and represents the best imaging response observed in this patient's disease course.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Mr. Delacroix is a 71-year-old gentleman with heavily pretreated mCRPC (post-abiraterone, post-enzalutamide, post-palliative EBRT) with germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant who has now completed 4 months on olaparib 200 mg BID (dose-reduced from 300 mg BID for grade 3 anemia). His oncologic response is excellent — PSA has declined to 1.4 ng/mL from a pre-treatment value of 9.2 ng/mL, and PSMA PET demonstrates marked reduction in metabolic disease activity without evidence of new visceral or nodal involvement. This is gratifying and consistent with the depth of response seen in BRCA2-mutated mCRPC treated on the PROfound trial.

However, he remains anemia-dependent with a current Hgb of 9.2 g/dL, having received 4 pRBC transfusions over the past 3 months. The anemia pattern is hypoproliferative (low reticulocyte count, normocytic, EPO-replete, iron-sufficient), consistent with olaparib-related bone marrow suppression. There is no evidence of hemolysis or nutritional deficiency as contributing factors. Platelets remain adequate though trending down.

1. mCRPC on olaparib (Lynparza) 200 mg BID:
   - Continue olaparib at current dose of 200 mg BID. Given the depth of PSA response and excellent PSMA PET findings, the benefit-risk ratio strongly favors continuing at the current dose rather than further reduction or discontinuation. Further dose reduction to 100 mg BID remains an option if grade 3 anemia recurs at the current dose, though this approaches the lower bound of therapeutic efficacy.
   - Continue ADT with leuprolide (Lupron) 22.5 mg IM — next injection due in approximately 6 weeks. Testosterone to be rechecked at that visit to confirm ongoing castration.
   - Continue zoledronic acid (Zometa) 4 mg IV quarterly for bone-protective therapy. Dental clearance is current. Creatinine check prior to each infusion (eGFR 64 currently, dose adjustment not required but monitoring important).

2. Transfusion-dependent anemia (grade 2, ongoing; grade 3 episodes treated):
   - Hgb 9.2 g/dL today, stable. Transfusion threshold: will transfuse for symptomatic anemia or Hgb <8 g/dL.
   - Will continue CBC every 2 weeks given ongoing myelosuppression risk.
   - Iron supplementation continued (ferrous sulfate 325 mg BID, though iron stores are adequate — continued for prophylaxis).
   - Erythropoiesis-stimulating agent (ESA) formally discussed today: given the hypoproliferative, EPO-replete pattern, darbepoetin alfa could potentially reduce transfusion burden. However, given the oncologic context (active malignancy, non-curative intent) and the patient's preference to minimize medications, the decision is to defer ESA for now and reassess if transfusion frequency increases. This was a shared decision-making discussion.
   - Patient counseled to report symptoms of symptomatic anemia (dyspnea at rest, chest pain, presyncope) immediately.
   - Will monitor platelets closely — currently 164 x10^9/L; if platelet count falls below 75 x10^9/L, will consider further dose modification per prescribing information.

3. Bone metastases and skeletal health:
   - Thoracic back pain remains well-controlled post-EBRT (T10-T12). Pain rated 1-2/10 today.
   - Zometa continued as above.
   - Vitamin D and calcium supplementation ongoing (calcium carbonate 1200 mg daily, vitamin D3 1000 IU daily).
   - No new neurological symptoms to suggest cord compromise at this time. Patient instructed to return to ED immediately or call 911 if he develops new bilateral leg weakness, numbness, or loss of bowel/bladder control — reviewed emergency signs of cord compression.

4. Hypertension:
   - Amlodipine 5 mg daily continued. BP 138/82 today — well-controlled. Continue as is.

5. Symptom management and quality of life:
   - Fatigue: Grade 2, multifactorial (ADT, anemia, cancer burden, deconditioning). Gentle activity as tolerated encouraged. Physical therapy referral renewed for fall prevention and conditioning.
   - Nausea: Managed with ondansetron PRN — patient reports this is adequate.
   - Hot flashes: Grade 2, persistent — patient has declined pharmacologic management to date; discussed venlafaxine as an option. Patient will consider.
   - Palliative care team continues to co-manage. Next palliative care appointment in 3 weeks.
   - Goals of care: Ongoing conversations with patient and wife. Patient understands disease is incurable. His stated priorities are maintaining quality of life, staying at home, and having adequate time with family. These align with continuing disease-directed therapy as long as tolerated.

6. Monitoring and next visit:
   - PSA, CBC with differential, CMP in 4 weeks.
   - CBC every 2 weeks as above (can be drawn at local lab).
   - CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast will be scheduled at approximately 8 months on olaparib (approximately 4 months from today) for formal restaging.
   - Return to clinic in 4-6 weeks or sooner PRN for transfusion, toxicity, or new symptoms.

Total visit time: approximately 45 minutes. Greater than 50% of the visit was spent in counseling and coordination of care, including anemia management discussion, goals of care, and interpretation of PSMA PET/CT results with the patient and his wife.

[Electronically signed by Attending Oncologist]
